Q1i.scenario: An insurer has four lines of health business: Individual IP (mean=85cr, SD=25cr), Group CI (mean=75cr, SD=30cr), LTCI (mean=90cr, SD=5cr), and PMI (mean=75cr, SD=15cr). It is considering stop-loss reinsurance and has asked you to rank the products by stop-loss premium for three different treaty structures.
Q1i.question: Rank the four products by stop-loss premium for: (a) XS 70 crores; (b) XS 100 crores; (c) XS 90 crores capped at 25 crores. [6 each = 18]

a) XS 70 crores: LTCI will have the highest probability of making a recovery under this contract — for a symmetrical distribution the chance of exceeding the mean is 50%. For LTCI, 70 cr is well below the mean of 90 cr, so recovery is highly likely.
For IP or CI, 70 cr is about 0.2 standard deviations below the mean, making recovery a little less likely than for LTCI.
However, 90 cr is only 1 SD above the mean for IP/CI, and 2 SD above is 115 cr/135 cr. This shows that the chance of a large recovery is greater for IP or CI than for LTCI.

PMI makes a recovery when the result is 0.33 standard deviations below the mean — slightly less likely than IP or CI. However, 2 SD above the mean is 105 cr for PMI, showing somewhat greater chance of large recovery than IP/CI.

It seems clear that LTCI will have the smallest stop-loss premium because it is much less variable (SD only 5 cr). The overall result between PMI and IP/CI is a close call depending on the assumed distribution of the loss ratio.
Ranking (highest to lowest premium): PMI ≈ IP/CI > LTCI.
b) XS 100 crores: PMI will have the greatest chance of any recovery (100 cr is 1.67 SD above mean of 75 cr) and also the greatest chance of a large recovery.
IP or CI will have the next greatest chance (100 cr is about 0.6 SD above mean for IP, 0.83 SD above mean for CI).
LTCI will have the least chance of any recovery (100 cr is 2 SD above mean of 90 cr) and also the least chance of a large recovery.
Ranking (highest to lowest premium): PMI > CI > IP > LTCI.
c) XS 90 crores capped at 25 crores: In terms of standard deviations above the mean, the layer runs: IP: +1.0 to +2.0; CI: +0.5 to +1.67; LTCI: +0.0 to +3.6 (but very unlikely to reach upper); PMI: +1.0 to +1.8 (cap reached within 1.8 SD).
Whenever the upper limit of this layer is breached (likeliest for PMI given its volatility relative to the layer), a full recovery of 25 cr is made.
It is clear that the premium order must be the same as in (b): PMI should have the largest premium, CI next, IP next and LTCI has the smallest.
Using a statistical package we can get the pure premiums by fitting suitable distributions (log-Normal, Gamma etc.) with the same moments as the empirical distributions.

Q1ii.scenario: Same as Q1(i). You have been asked to price the stop-loss reinsurance using burning cost methodology.
Q1ii.question: Calculate the burning cost pure premiums for each product and each treaty structure, and comment on the results compared to the analytical approach in Q1(i).

Burning cost calculation: The burning cost pure premiums are the average past recoveries if the cover had been in place throughout: (XS 70 cr) IP: 5.8 cr; CI: 7.4 cr; LTCI: 4.4 cr; PMI: 8 cr. (XS 100 cr) IP: 0 cr; CI: 1.4 cr; LTCI: 0 cr; PMI: 0.8 cr. (XS 90 cr, capped 25 cr) IP: 0 cr; CI: 3.4 cr; LTCI: 0 cr; PMI: 2.8 cr.
These are dramatically different from the results in part (i). CI always has a much larger burning cost and hence should have higher premium than IP.
PMI is not the largest for XS 100 cr or XS 90 cr capped at 25 cr — CI is. This contradicts the analytical approach.

IP and LTCI each have zero burning cost for XS 100 cr and XS 90 cr (capped at 25 cr) — implying zero premium, which seems inappropriate as there is presumably still some probability of a recovery being made in the future.
Limitations of burning cost vs analytica: Clearly neither the analytical approach alone, nor the burning cost alone, provides a suitable way to rate a stop-loss treaty.

The analytical approach penalises IP relative to CI, although past experience suggests that when CI makes a loss at all it makes a relatively big loss — which is exactly what unlimited or high-layer stop-loss cover is protecting against.
It seems inappropriate to have a zero pure premium for XS 100 cr or XS 90 cr capped 25 cr to IP or LTCI, despite there presumably being some chance that a recovery would be made in future.

The ideal is to decide what the prospective distribution of the total claims for any line of business is, then calculate the pure premium for any layer directly from that distribution. To make as good a job as possible, we would study all relevant historical information for the risk and also all others that could shed light on the distribution.

Key adjustments needed: changes in the precise cover provided; the effect of inflation and the measure which correctly accounts for the mix of past claims and next year's prospective claims; the business cycle (e.g. if a portfolio increases next year by 10%, claims will increase by a factor close to that).

The ideal practical approach would probably be to do as much as possible analytically, then pass those results to a highly skilled health underwriter for them to estimate a suitable risk premium, taking the analytical results as part of their deliberations.

Q1iii.scenario: Same as Q1(i-ii). You are advising on the overall approach to rating a stop-loss treaty.
Q1iii.question: Describe the ideal approach to rating a stop-loss reinsurance treaty.

Ideal approach to stop-loss rating: Clearly neither the burning cost approach alone, nor the analytical approach alone, provides a suitable way to rate a stop-loss treaty.

The ideal would be to decide what the prospective distribution of the total claims for any line of business is, and then calculate the pure premium for any layer of cover directly from that distribution — whether it is an analytical or empirical distribution, using a suitable computer package.
To make as good a job as possible of this, we would study all relevant historical information for the risk, and also all others that could shed light on the distribution for this risk.

We would need to be careful about the effects of changes in: the precise cover provided (if it is reducing, future claim amounts will not be as bad as past ones); the effect of inflation and the measure which correctly accounts for the mix of past claims and next year's prospective claims from this portfolio; the business cycle (e.g. if a portfolio increases next year by 10%, claims next year will increase by a factor close to that).

The ideal practical approach would probably be to do as much as possible analytically, then look at the result on various bases (including burning cost and analytical distributions), then pass those results to a highly skilled health underwriter for them to estimate a suitable risk premium, taking the analytical results as part of their deliberations.

Q1iv.scenario: An insurer is considering applying a matching adjustment to its discount rate for a block of long-term care insurance (LTCI) business. On 50% of the backing assets, an additional yield of 1.50% is available. The default risk on these assets is estimated at 0.50%.
Q1iv.question: Q1(iv) State the conditions required to apply a matching adjustment and calculate the extent of the matching adjustment. [5]

Matching adjustment conditions and calcu: Conditions for applying a matching adjustment: (a) Must be approved by the regulator; (b) Long-term predictable liabilities — no surrender is allowed; (c) The key liability risks are longevity and expense; (d) Assets are ring-fenced and no pre-mature redemption is allowed; (e) The assets (bonds) are matched by nature, term and currency of the expected benefits.

Calculation: On 50% of the backing assets, 1.50% of additional yield is available. Excluding 0.50% for default risk, the overall additional yield = 50% × (1.50% − 0.50%) = 0.50%. Assuming the term of the assets and liabilities are matched, the extent of the matching adjustment is 0.50% p.a.

Q1v.scenario: An insurer has acquired a block of unit-linked LTCI business. You have been asked to prepare an EV calculation for this block.
Q1v.question: Describe the method for calculating the Embedded Value of the acquired LTCI business.

Net assets: The net assets will be the total assets in excess of those covering the technical provisions plus SCR under Solvency II.
As the business is 100% shareholder-owned, 100% of the market value of these net assets will be included in the embedded value (EV).
PVIF (Present Value of In-Force): The PVIF is the present value of future shareholder cashflows projected to emerge from the assets backing the liabilities.

Under Solvency II, the PVIF includes the release of the risk margin component of the technical provisions and the release of the SCR. These amounts may be reduced to allow for the cost of holding this required capital (i.e. frictional costs such as taxation and agency costs).

The PVIF also represents the release of any margins if there are places where the assumptions underlying the best estimate liabilities are not in line with the company's best estimate assumptions — for example, if the best estimate liabilities have been determined using a constrained discount rate or including premiums up to some restrictive contract boundary.

To calculate the PVIF, a model would be used to project future cashflows on the business and the future supervisory reserves and capital requirements. In the case of the unit-linked LTCI business, both the unit and non-unit funds would be projected, as the non-unit cashflows depend on the size of the unit fund.

A set of assumptions will be needed to project the future cashflows, including investment returns, mortality, expenses, withdrawals, inflation and so on. The current and likely future tax position of the company must also be considered — tax should therefore be dealt with at a global level.
Model points and discount rate: The in-force policies may be modelled individually, but this may not be practical due to IT limitations or limited access to data. The in-force business could then be represented by a number of sample policies (model points). The contracts will be split by class (unit-linked and without-profits treated separately), then into smaller groups by product type, policy term, sales method etc.
The PVIF will be the present value of the shareholder transfer arising each year. The discount rate may allow for a risk margin.

If the embedded value calculation is performed on a market-consistent basis, the investment return and discount rate assumptions will be based on risk-free rates, and separate allowance would need to be made to allow for non-hedgeable risks.

Q1vi.scenario: Same as Q1(v). The unit-linked LTCI policies have a provision for top-up premiums which act as recurrent single premiums. The question arises as to how future top-up premiums should be treated in the EV calculation.
Q1vi.question: Discuss how future top-up premiums should be treated in the EV calculation.

Treatment of top-up premiums in EV: Because the contracts have the provision of top-up premiums which act as recurrent single premiums, it is difficult to judge what future premiums under the contracts are likely to be. There is scope for discussion as to whether the future premiums should be regarded as part of the in-force business.

This is similar to the issue of establishing the contract boundary for these contracts for Solvency II purposes — although a different treatment may be adopted for this embedded value (where the treatment is unconstrained) than for Solvency II (which must comply with the regulations).

Possible approaches: (1) Treat as new business and therefore ignore future contributions to in-force business — the purchaser might prefer this, but may be difficult to justify; however, if this is the treatment adopted by the company under Solvency II, this may strengthen the argument. (2) Allow for future premiums on some assumptions — these may be best estimate, although the purchaser may prefer prudent assumptions and might assume high rates of withdrawal and of ceasing contributions.

Q2i.scenario: WTH is a life and health insurance company that has been offering health insurance products for the last five years in Country A. WTH is now planning to launch an individual Income Protection (IP) product in Country A, where IP insurance is currently not available. WTH is also planning to enter Country B with a similar product.
Q2i.question: Outline the typical key features of an individual income protection insurance product.

Key features of individual IP: Provides insured with regular income during periods when the insured is unable to work due to illness or incapacity. The policy may have several exclusions such that not all illnesses/injuries may be covered (e.g. mental illness).

Policy term typically runs up to retirement (e.g. age 60) but could be for a fixed term. Regular premiums paid by insured (e.g. yearly, half-yearly, quarterly or monthly). Premiums can be level or increasing in line with an index. Waiver of premium whilst claim is in payment.

There can be multiple periods of sickness benefits paid during policy term. Typically includes a deferred period — e.g. a policy with a 4-week deferred period will commence income payments once the policyholder has been off work for 4 weeks.
Typically includes a linked claims period — after a claim has ended, if the policyholder goes off work again for the same reason within, say, a 12-month period, a new deferred period will not apply.

Benefits can be level or increasing in line with an index. Benefits could escalate differently in and out of claim. Benefits limited to prevent over-insurance — limit based on a percentage of salary (gross or net of tax) at date of incapacity. Benefits may reduce or cease after a specified period.
Definitions of incapacity — occupation based (e.g. unable to do 'own' occupation) or activity based (e.g. unable to carry out 4 out of 6 activities of daily living such as washing, bathing etc.).
There may be rehabilitation/counselling services to facilitate earlier return to work.

Q2ii.scenario: Same as Q2(i). WTH is deciding whether to offer guaranteed or reviewable premium rates for the IP product.
Q2ii.question: Explain the advantages and disadvantages to WTH of offering guaranteed premium rates for the IP product.

Advantages of guaranteed premium rates: Increase in sales as policyholders would like certainty in terms of the premium payable throughout the term of the policy — particularly the case if the competition is not offering guaranteed rates and WTH's pricing is not out of line with the market.
No risk of bad publicity and reputational risks associated with reviewable rates. Likely lower level of regulatory scrutiny compared to reviewable rates.

Less issue of selective lapsation — since the premium rate is fixed there is no direct trigger for policyholders to be actively looking for better rates. More certainty with respect to the lapse assumption compared to reviewable rates.
System administration is easier and less expensive compared to reviewable rates.
Disadvantages of guaranteed premium rate: There will be heavy reliance on the sufficiency of pricing data and credibility of experience at outset, as the insurer does not have the option to fine-tune premium rates on written business in the future.
Profitability may decrease as the company does not have the option to change premiums if actual experience turns out to be worse than the original pricing assumptions.
Reinsurance — it may be difficult to obtain reinsurance as some reinsurers in the market may not offer guaranteed terms.

Reserving requirements for guaranteed business are likely to be higher than those for reviewable business as there is no option to change premium rates. Capital requirements are similarly likely to be higher.
Decrease in sales if the premium is higher due to higher reserving/capital requirements — particularly the case if competition is offering significantly lower rates with a reviewable structure.

Q2iii.scenario: Same as Q2(i). WTH is considering offering an 'accident-only' variant of the IP product.
Q2iii.question: Outline the possible reasons why WTH may consider offering an accident-only IP product, and the risks associated with such a product.

Possible reasons for accident-only IP: Better alignment to need for certain categories of target market — e.g. young lives in blue collar industries.

Significant claim adjudication challenges with respect to sickness disabilities. Bad publicity/repudiation risk due to claim declinature for sickness claims leading to disgruntled policyholders. Insurance ombudsman/court rulings against the insurer for most sickness IP claims.
Claim adjudication is more objective/easier for injuries than sickness. Simplified underwriting which is a main hurdle for sales.
Long-term guarantee is less risky with 'accident only' vs 'accident plus sickness'. Less exposure to epidemics and pandemics.
Risks of accident-only IP: Accident and sickness gets blurred for certain disabilities — e.g. falling due to sickness and getting hurt, road accidents caused by fatigue due to sickness. Risk of having to pay claims not envisaged at pricing. Risk of bad publicity/repudiation if claims are denied in such instances.
Anti-selection — those with greater exposure to accidents picking up the cover. Underwriting might help to an extent but would defeat the objective of simplified issue.
Risk of pricing mis-estimation in the absence of incidence and termination data that clearly splits out cause of disability, occupation, industry, age, gender etc.

Risk of decreased profitability if actual target segment is different from expected. Selective lapsation from the current product and entry into this product, causing profitability challenges for other products. Distribution churning from existing product into the new product since first year commission tends to be higher than renewal commission.

Q2iv.scenario: Same as Q2(i). WTH is considering entering Country B with an IP product. Country B has no existing IP insurance market.
Q2iv.question: Describe the factors that should be considered by WTH before deciding on whether to enter Country B with an IP product.

Demand and competition: Would there be sufficient demand to sell volumes? No state provision and no IP insurance means this is a greenfield opportunity with potential for sufficient volume of sales.

Are there personal tax incentives that might make the product more attractive? How aware are customers of the need for IP? How are they currently addressing the need? Would the product be affordable? Would carry out a survey to assess these. New company in the market — consumer confidence in the company/brand and in the product.

Any previous attempts from outside companies — if so, why did they not enter? Generally, how profitable is the health insurance market in Country B — if existing insurers (though with different products) are not making money, why should WTH?
Data and capital/regulatory: Availability of relevant and sufficient data to price — disability incidence, termination split by risk factors such as age, gender, occupation, income etc.
Licensing requirements. Capital/solvency requirements. Expense overruns/new business strain. Reserving/financial reporting requirements. Restrictions on product design/investments.
Regulations of distributors (e.g. commission levels)/sales process. Contract law/employment law. How evolved are the insurance ombudsman/insurance legal systems?
Distribution channels and talent: Availability of qualified salespeople/training required. Potential channels and how evolved they are — own sales force, bancassurance, independent advice, direct to customer.
Availability/hiring of key staff. Administrative and claims systems. Availability of service partners (e.g. disability rehabilitation/counselling). Possibilities for outsourcing.
Profitability and other factors: Demographics/demand projections. Profitability levels and projected growth. Investment returns. Regulatory limitations on profitability. Cultural impacts on propensity to claim/lapse. Repatriation of profits. Corporate tax.

Willingness of reinsurers to support business. Health care infrastructure — hospitals and doctors (referrals, reports) — how does this product's underwriting and claim processing fit with that infrastructure?
Potential for fraud. Modelling and profit testing. Would buying fully/partially an existing insurer in Country B be a better alternative?

Q2v.scenario: Same as Q2(i). WTH is comparing the rating factors it would use for the IP product in Country A (where it has five years of experience) versus Country B (a new market).
Q2v.question: Explain the differences in rating factors WTH would use for the IP product in Country A versus Country B.

Rating factor differences A vs B: Country A is more matured/developed and so will have adequate data/experience for segmented pricing — therefore will tend to have a higher number of rating factors than Country B.
Typical rating factors for IP include: age; gender; occupation; location; income level; type of disability definition; length of deferred period; level of replacement ratio.

Given Country B is new to IP, the following will likely inhibit segmented pricing: data limitations (availability and quality); need to keep the rating structure simple to make it easier for distribution to sell; need to keep the rating structure simple for customers to understand.

Also, given the riskiness with any new product with no locally relevant experience, WTH will be reluctant to offer certain features and hence premium rating factors — for example, higher replacement ratio or 'own occupation' disability definition.

In both countries, regulations may limit the rating factors that can be applied — for example, no gender discrimination; no discrimination due to place of residence of the policyholder within the country.

Q2vi.scenario: Same as Q2(i). WTH has decided to launch the IP product in Country B with a reviewable premium structure — specifically, premiums guaranteed for the first 5 years and reviewable thereafter.
Q2vi.question: Suggest reasons why WTH has decided on a reviewable premium rate structure with a 5-year initial guarantee for the Country B product.

Reasons for reviewable rates with 5-year: Market research has shown there is a viable market opportunity. Higher internet adaptability for buying health insurance online and so website distribution. Decided to target younger lives and so internet distribution — due to lower risk and more incentive to return to work.
Other types of distribution not making economic sense due to higher costs and lack of available qualified resources.

Onerous capital requirements for full guarantees and so reviewable rates are preferred. Lack of relevant data makes a reviewable proposition a less risky way to start. Market is used to reviewable health insurance and so the approach is aligned.

Research/survey has shown some initial period of guarantee is important to gain traction for the product. The '5-years' initial guarantee period allows enough data to be gathered before the premium review.
Regulations require at least 5-years guarantee. The initial guarantee likely encourages good early-duration persistency when asset share is negative.

Q2vii.scenario: Same as Q2(vi). WTH has been writing the IP product in Country B for 5 years and is now carrying out its first premium review.
Q2vii.question: Describe the premium review process WTH should follow.

Experience investigation: Morbidity experience data should be collected since the last pricing or premium review date, whichever is the latest.

Incidence rates and termination rates will be looked at for each homogeneous group split by: age; gender; occupation (if known); location/area of residence; income level; duration from entry; underwriting method; income replacement ratio; type of disability definition; length of waiting period.

Mortality experience is also an important component of the investigation. Analysis of type of claim (accident or sickness), and ideally subdivided into causes — type of accident/sickness that led to disability.

Exposure calculation — important to ensure that only lives insured in the investigation period under consideration are included. Equally important to ensure that claims are grouped so that the exposure and claims correspond. Adjustments to 'exposed to risk' will need to be made for withdrawals (lapse/free-look cancellation), on a life-by-life basis using the date of entry and/or exit.

A vs E — the insurer needs to monitor the actual claims experience against the expected level, by issue year (to reflect underwriting implications) and by calendar year (to reflect the general claims environment).

Expense analysis — monitor actual expenses incurred against expected level, split by initial, renewal and claims expenses, allowing for inflation since the last analysis and excluding any one-off expenses.

Lapse investigation — actual to expected lapses, by duration given the long-term nature of the product. Investment income — actual to expected (though with regular premiums, the significance of investment income is likely to be low).
Assumption revision and profit testing: The best estimate assumptions will need to be revised based on the results of the experience investigations, allowing for sufficiency and credibility of own data, and making appropriate allowance for expectations of future trends in incidence and termination rates.

The risk discount rate will be based on the insurer's required profit margin at the original pricing date, but the risk-free rate should be consistent with current economic conditions. Investment return will need to be consistent with the risk discount rate and expense inflation.

For each policy/model point, cashflows will be projected forward from the premium review date to the end of the policy term. The net projected cashflows will then be discounted at the risk discount rate. The profitability criterion based on the latest assumptions should be compared with that used in the last review.

This will allow WTH to decide if the rates should be adjusted to maintain the level of profitability of the policies concerned when they were priced. Consider if there is scope to reduce rates based on the outcome of the analysis if excess profits are expected.

Consider how the results of any review are communicated to policyholders. Ensure the policyholders are given sufficient time to make decisions about whether to continue with cover, particularly when rates are increased.

Weight the risk of selective withdrawal and potential impact on volume to cover overheads if rates are increased. Consider impact of any changes that could be made to the underwriting/claims/other processes before deciding on the rate increase — rate increase is the last resort if nothing else can be tweaked to improve the experience.
